%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.6-2 Matrix Algebra

> **Section Objective:** Learn when core matrix operations are legal, how to compute them, and which dimension checks must be done first.

## Concepts In This Section
- matrix addition
- scalar multiplication
- matrix multiplication
- conformability
- identity matrix
- inverse matrix

## 1. Addition and Scalar Multiplication

Addition is entry-by-entry and needs the same order.

$$A + B = [a_{ij} + b_{ij}]$$

**Minimal example:** a \(2\times 3\) matrix can be added only to another \(2\times 3\) matrix.

Scalar multiplication scales every entry.

$$cA = Ac$$

**Minimal example:** if \(A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4\end{bmatrix}\), then \(3A = \begin{bmatrix}3 & 6 \\ 9 & 12\end{bmatrix}\).

## 2. Matrix Multiplication

Matrix multiplication uses the row-by-column rule.

$$c_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^n a_{ik} b_{kj}$$

The product \(AB\) is defined only when the number of columns of \(A\) equals the number of rows of \(B\).

**Minimal example:** if \(A\) is \(2\times 3\) and \(B\) is \(3\times 4\), then \(AB\) is defined.

## 3. Conformability and Output Size

Check the inner dimensions first. If they match, the output size comes from the outer dimensions.

$$A_{m\times n} B_{n\times p} \Rightarrow AB_{m\times p}$$

**Minimal example:** \((2\times 3)(3\times 5)\) is valid and gives a \(2\times 5\) result.

## 4. AB Need Not Equal BA

Order matters in matrix multiplication.

**Minimal example:** if \(A\) is \(2\times 3\) and \(B\) is \(3\times 4\), then \(AB\) exists but \(BA\) does not.

## 5. Identity Matrix

The identity leaves a compatible matrix unchanged.

$$AI = IA = A$$

**Minimal example:** multiplying a \(3\times 3\) matrix by \(I_3\) returns the same matrix.

## 6. Inverse Matrix

An inverse undoes a nonsingular square matrix.

$$A^{-1}A = AA^{-1} = I$$

**Minimal example:** if \(Ax = y\) and \(A\) is invertible, then \(x = A^{-1}y\).

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Addition needs the same order.
- Multiplication needs matching inner dimensions.
- Output size comes from outer dimensions.
- Identity preserves a matrix; inverse undoes it when it exists.
